content.text:
<timestamp>Thursday, May 7, 2026, 4:18 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
GCP and TheOhanaWay: I created a GCP instance using my business email, but it may not have been connected to the business because I don't see a way to pay with the business card.
</user_query>

This is a really common GCP gotcha — signing in with a business email address doesn't automatically tie the account to your business. Here's what's actually going on and how to fix it.

## The three things GCP keeps separate

1. **Google Account / identity** — the email you sign in with (`you@theohanaway.com` or `you@gmail.com`).
2. **Cloud Billing account** — the payment profile (card on file, who gets invoiced, business vs. individual).
3. **Project / Organization** — the container that holds your VM and other resources, which gets *attached* to a billing account.

You can sign in with a business email but still have a **personal/individual** billing account underneath, which is almost certainly what happened. The card on file is determined by the **payments profile type** (Individual vs. Business), not the email address.

## How to check what you have

Go to [console.cloud.google.com/billing](https://console.cloud.google.com/billing) and click your billing account, then **Payment settings**. Look at the **Account type**:

- **Individual** → invoiced/charged to a person. You can't add a business card with the business as the legal payer here. This profile type cannot be changed after creation.
- **Business** → invoiced to the business name + address, accepts business cards, can have multiple admins, supports tax ID / EIN.

Also check [payments.google.com](https://payments.google.com) — that's where the underlying payments profile lives (it's shared across Google services, not just GCP).

## How to fix it (the right way)

Since account type is **immutable**, you generally need to create a new Business billing account and migrate:

1. **Create a new Cloud Billing account** at [console.cloud.google.com/billing/create](https://console.cloud.google.com/billing/create).
   - When prompted for **Account type**, choose **Business**.
   - Enter The Ohana Way's legal name, business address, and (optionally) tax ID.
   - Add the business credit card as the payment method.
2. **Move the project to the new billing account**:
   - Billing → click the *new* billing account → **Account management** → **My projects** (or go to the project's **Billing** page) → **Change billing**.
3. **Optionally close the old (Individual) billing account** once nothing is attached to it.

## Bonus: do you want a GCP Organization?

If The Ohana Way uses **Google Workspace** (i.e., `@theohanaway.com` is a Workspace domain you control), you can also set up a GCP **Organization** resource, which gives you proper IAM, project hierarchy, and ownership tied to the business domain rather than to your personal login. This is worth doing if more than one person will ever touch GCP, or if you want continuity if you leave the Google account behind. See [cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-organization](https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-organization).

If `@theohanaway.com` is *not* on Workspace (e.g., it's just an alias forwarded from another mail provider), you can't create a GCP Organization — you'd just stick with a Business billing account, which is still fine for paying with the business card and getting business invoices.

## What I'd do in your shoes

1. Open the billing console and confirm whether the existing profile is **Individual** or **Business**.
2. If Individual → create a new **Business** billing account, attach the business card, then reassign the project. Takes ~10 minutes.
3. If you're on Workspace and plan to grow this → also set up the **Organization** so everything is owned by the business, not by your personal login.

Want me to write this up as a short ops note in `docs/` (e.g., `docs/infrastructure.md` or a new `docs/gcp-setup.md`) so future-you and any agents working in this repo know how the GCP billing is structured? It would also be a good place to record the project ID, billing account ID, and which services are running where.

 The user is asking about a GCP billing issue—they set up an instance with their business email but aren't sure if it's properly connected to their business account since they can't find a way to pay with their business card. I should walk them through how GCP billing works and guide them on connecting a business payment method rather than jumping to tools.

The key distinction here is that a GCP project and billing account are separate entities. Just because they signed in with a business email doesn't mean the billing is set up for the business—the billing account itself determines what payment method gets charged. To use a business card, they'll likely need to either set up a billing account with the business as the payer, potentially link it to a Google Workspace organization, or ensure their business email is properly associated with the right billing account. I'll give them clear, actionable steps to figure out which scenario applies to them.

<timestamp>Thursday, May 7, 2026, 4:51 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
In Account Type is says "Organization"
</user_query>

That’s a good sign. In Google payments, **Account type: Organization** usually means the payments profile is already a business/organization profile, not an individual one.

So the issue is probably one of these:

- You may not have permission to manage payment methods.
- The business card may need to be added in **Google Payments Center**, not directly in the GCP project screen.
- The VM/project may be attached to a different billing account than the one you’re viewing.
- The card type/address/verification may be blocked or pending.

Check these in order:

1. Go to [payments.google.com](https://payments.google.com) and switch to the same payments profile used by GCP.
2. Open **Payment methods** and see whether you can add the business card there.
3. In GCP, go to **Billing** → select the billing account → **Account management** → confirm your role includes **Billing Account Administrator** or **Payments Profile Admin**.
4. In the GCP project, open **Billing** and confirm it is linked to that organization billing account.

If it says **Organization**, I would not create a new billing account yet. First confirm whether your project is attached to the correct billing account and whether your user has payment-management permissions.

<timestamp>Thursday, May 7, 2026, 4:55 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
In step 2, I could add a business card there. However, there is a business card in the admin and I was hoping to link the GCP payment to that method. Is that possible?
</user_query>

Short answer: **the card itself can't be "shared" across profiles, but the same card can absolutely be added to the GCP payments profile** — and if you set things up right, both Workspace and GCP can live under the *same* payments profile and see the same list of cards.

## Why you're seeing two separate lists

Google's payments system has the concept of a **payments profile**. Each profile owns its own list of payment methods. The card sitting in the **Google Workspace Admin Console** (admin.google.com → Billing) belongs to the *Workspace* payments profile. Your GCP billing account is attached to a *Cloud* payments profile, which may or may not be the same one.

If they're different profiles, the card from Workspace simply won't appear in the GCP dropdown — even though the same business "owns" both. Google doesn't auto-link them.

## Three ways to handle it

**Option 1 (easiest, what most people do):** Just re-add the same card to the GCP payments profile. You already confirmed in the previous step that you can add a card there. Add it, set it as default, done. Two profiles, same card number, same charges go to the same business account.

**Option 2 (cleanest if possible):** Move GCP billing onto the Workspace payments profile so both share one profile. To check if that's even an option:

1. In [payments.google.com](https://payments.google.com), use the profile-switcher (top-left dropdown) and see how many profiles you have access to.
2. If you see both an "Organization" profile (used by GCP) and a separate Workspace one, they're distinct.
3. Whether they can be merged depends on the profile types — Google generally **does not allow merging** payments profiles after creation, so this often isn't actually doable. Don't lose sleep over it.

**Option 3:** Create a new GCP billing account that uses the **Workspace payments profile** as its payment source (only available if both are on the same organization in your Google Workspace setup), then reassign your project to it. This is the "proper" path if you're doing a lot of GCP and want one consolidated bill, but it's overkill for one VM.

## My recommendation

For a single GCP instance, **just add the same business card to the GCP payments profile (Option 1)**. It's two minutes of work, both Workspace and GCP charges hit the same physical card, and you get separate but cleanly-organized invoices for "Workspace" and "Cloud" — which is actually nice for accounting.

If you'd like, I can write up a short note in `docs/infrastructure.md` capturing:
- Which payments profile owns GCP billing
- Which owns Workspace billing
- That they're separate-but-same-card by design

…so you (and any future agents working in the repo) don't have to re-derive this in six months.

 The user is asking if they can use a business card already linked to their Google Workspace Admin Console as a payment method for GCP. The key distinction here is that Workspace and Google Cloud maintain separate payment profiles by default, so payment methods don't automatically transfer between them—though there may be ways to connect them.

The real answer depends on whether both services are using the same organization payments profile. If they are, the card should already be available in both places. If not, they'd need to either switch GCP's billing to use the Workspace payments profile, or manually add the same card to the GCP billing account's payment methods.

<timestamp>Thursday, May 7, 2026, 4:58 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
I want to do option 3. Can you provide the steps?

I am an admin but not super admin. I am working to become super admin because I need that to setup IAM.
</user_query>

Yes. Option 3 is: create a **new Google Cloud Billing account** under the business/Workspace organization, using the same **organization payments profile** that already has the business card, then move the GCP project/VM onto that new billing account.

Important caveat: this only works if Google lets you select the existing Workspace payments profile during Cloud Billing setup. If it does not appear, you may need Super Admin or Payments Profile Admin access, or Google may not allow that profile to be reused for Cloud Billing.

## Prerequisites

You likely need one or more of these:

- **Google Workspace Super Admin**: needed for organization-level control and IAM setup.
- **Payments Profile Admin** on the business payments profile: needed to manage/reuse the card/payment profile.
- **Billing Account Creator** on the Google Cloud organization: needed to create a new Cloud Billing account.
- **Project Billing Manager** or Owner on the existing GCP project: needed to switch the project to the new billing account.

Since you are not Super Admin yet, you may be blocked at the org/IAM step.

## Steps

1. **Confirm the Workspace payments profile**
   - Go to [payments.google.com](https://payments.google.com).
   - Use the profile selector.
   - Find the business/Workspace payments profile that already has the business card.
   - Confirm it says **Organization** and that the card is listed under **Payment methods**.

2. **Confirm your Google Cloud organization exists**
   - Go to [Google Cloud Console](https://console.cloud.google.com).
   - Open the project/resource selector at the top.
   - Look for an organization named something like **TheOhanaWay** or your Workspace domain.
   - If you only see “No organization,” the Workspace/GCP organization may not be set up or you may not have access.

3. **Create a new Cloud Billing account**
   - Go to [Cloud Billing](https://console.cloud.google.com/billing).
   - Click **Create account**.
   - For organization, choose the TheOhanaWay/Workspace organization.
   - For account type, choose **Organization** or business/organization equivalent.
   - When prompted for the payments profile, look for the existing Workspace/business payments profile.
   - Select that profile if available.
   - Finish creating the billing account.

4. **Set permissions on the new billing account**
   - In Cloud Billing, open the new billing account.
   - Go to **Account management** or **Permissions**.
   - Add yourself as **Billing Account Administrator**.
   - Add any future admins as needed.
   - Keep the business payments profile admins aligned with whoever handles finance.

5. **Move the GCP project to the new billing account**
   - In Cloud Console, open the project that contains your VM.
   - Go to **Billing**.
   - Click **Change billing**.
   - Select the new Cloud Billing account.
   - Save.

6. **Verify the VM is still running**
   - Go to **Compute Engine → VM instances**.
   - Confirm the instance is active.
   - Check **Billing → Reports** after a little time to confirm charges are flowing to the new billing account.

7. **Clean up the old billing account**
   - Once no projects are attached to the old billing account, leave it unused or close it.
   - Do not close it until the project has successfully moved and billing is active on the new account.

## If the Workspace payment profile does not appear

Most likely causes:

- You are not **Super Admin**.
- You are not **Payments Profile Admin**.
- You lack **Billing Account Creator** on the Cloud organization.
- Google does not allow that particular Workspace payments profile to be reused for Cloud Billing.

In that case, once you become Super Admin, first grant yourself the needed org/billing roles, then try the billing-account creation flow again. If it still does not appear, the practical fallback is to create a separate Cloud Billing account and add the same business card there.

**Clarifying GCP billing steps**

I need to provide an answer about Google Cloud Platform (GCP) billing accounts. It's essential to mention that a Google Workspace super admin is often required to create an organization. I'll outline the necessary steps carefully to avoid any mistakes. It's crucial to understand that Google Cloud and Workspace billing profiles might operate separately. Also, the Google Payments profile can be shared if it's the same legal entity and you have the necessary permissions. I’ll emphasize role assignments, especially for organization and IAM.
**Explaining billing account creation**

I need to clarify that to create a Cloud Billing account, the Billing Account Creator role is necessary within the organization. A Google Workspace super admin can grant this role. The answer needs to be concise yet informative, ensuring the user understands the requirements for setting up a billing account in GCP properly. Having the right permissions is key to making this process smooth and efficient.

<timestamp>Thursday, May 7, 2026, 5:03 PM (UTC-7)</timestamp>
<user_query>
In step 1, there is no profile selector. I assume that is because I am not a super admin, correct?

In step 3, there is not Workspace payments profile, again probably because I don't have rights.
</user_query>

Probably yes, but the underlying reason is more specific than "not Super Admin." It's worth knowing the distinction so you ask for the right thing when you become Super Admin.

## Workspace Super Admin ≠ Payments Profile Admin

Google has **two separate permission systems** here:

1. **Workspace Admin roles** (managed at admin.google.com) — controls Workspace, users, groups, and *Workspace billing*.
2. **Google Payments profile roles** (managed at payments.google.com) — controls who can see and manage a specific payments profile and its cards.

Being **Workspace Super Admin doesn't automatically make you a Payments Profile Admin.** They're related (a Super Admin usually has the ability to add themselves to the payments profile, or sees more entry points), but the actual permission lives on the payments profile itself.

## Why the profile selector is missing in step 1

The profile dropdown at payments.google.com only appears when **your Google account has access to more than one payments profile**. Right now you only see your own/personal profile, which means you have not been added to the business **Organization** payments profile.

To fix that, the current Super Admin (or whoever currently owns the business payments profile) needs to:

1. Sign in to [payments.google.com](https://payments.google.com).
2. Switch to the **Organization** payments profile (the one with the business card).
3. Go to **Settings → Payments users → Manage payments users**.
4. Add your business email with role **Admin** (or at minimum **Edit payments methods**).

Once that's done, **you'll see the profile selector**, and the business card will appear in your view.

## Why the Workspace payments profile is missing in step 3

When creating a new Cloud Billing account, the dropdown of available payments profiles is filtered to profiles where **you are a Payments Profile Admin AND the profile is eligible for Cloud Billing**. So this is the same root cause as above — you're not on that profile yet — plus the additional GCP-side requirements I mentioned (Billing Account Creator on the org, etc.).

## So what to ask for when you become Super Admin

You actually need three things, not just Super Admin:

1. **Workspace Super Admin** ← you're working on this
2. **Payments Profile Admin** on the business Organization payments profile (added at payments.google.com)
3. **Billing Account Creator** + **Organization Administrator** at the GCP organization level (added in GCP IAM)

Super Admin gives you the *power* to grant yourself #2 and #3, but they don't come automatically — you (or whoever has the rights now) still has to explicitly assign them.

## Quick thing you could try right now

Even without Super Admin, ask the current Super Admin to add you to the business payments profile (the steps under "Why the profile selector is missing" above — it takes them about 60 seconds). That alone will let you see and use the business card in step 1, and may surface the Workspace payments profile in step 3 depending on org-level GCP roles.

 I'm thinking through why the profile selector and Workspace payments profile might be missing. The profile selector only shows up when you have multiple payments profiles available, and being a Super Admin in Workspace doesn't automatically grant access to the payments profile itself—you need to be explicitly added as a user at the payments profile level with the appropriate permissions.

For the Cloud Billing account creation, the available payments profiles are filtered by both your admin access to them and whether they're eligible for Cloud Billing. A Workspace-only payments profile might not always qualify for Cloud Billing, and you'd need to be a Payments Profile Admin on that specific profile to use it. So this is really a permissions issue within Google Payments rather than a Super Admin limitation—the current Super Admin would need to add you as an Admin to the payments profile directly.
